title: Adult Vaccination Schedule and Awareness
source_name: IAP / API Adult Immunisation Guidelines + NHM India + WHO

content.overview:
Vaccination is one of the most cost-effective public health interventions, preventing 2–3 million deaths annually globally. While childhood immunisation receives most attention, adult vaccination is an underutilised prevention strategy in India. Adults remain susceptible to many vaccine-preventable diseases (VPDs) — either because childhood vaccines wane over time, vaccines were missed, or because new vaccines are now available. The Association of Physicians of India (API) and the Indian Academy of Pediatrics (IAP) have developed adult vaccination guidelines tailored to the Indian context.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for health awareness and general guidance only. Vaccination schedules should be individualised based on age, health status, occupation, travel plans, and vaccination history. Consult a qualified healthcare provider to determine the right vaccines for you. All vaccines — including those mentioned above — should be administered by trained healthcare professionals.

vaccination: Waning immunity: Some childhood vaccines (e.g. tetanus, pertussis) lose effectiveness over time and need boosters
Missed vaccines: Many adults in India were not vaccinated against certain diseases as children
New vaccines: Some vaccines (e.g. HPV, zoster) are newly available or newly recommended for adults
Life stage changes: Pregnancy, chronic illness, travel, and aging increase vulnerability to specific infections
Protecting others: Vaccinated adults reduce transmission to vulnerable contacts (infants, elderly, immunocompromised)
The following is for informational awareness; actual schedule should be decided by a doctor based on individual health status, prior vaccination history, and risk factors.
TETANUS, DIPHTHERIA, PERTUSSIS (Td / Tdap)
Tdap: One dose if never received as adult (replaces one Td booster)
Td: Booster every 10 years thereafter
Pregnant women: Tdap at every pregnancy (27–36 weeks) — protects newborn from pertussis
Tetanus: Also given post-wound/injury if not vaccinated in last 5 years
INFLUENZA (FLU VACCINE)
Annual vaccination recommended
High priority for: Adults >60 years, pregnant women, healthcare workers, those with chronic illness (diabetes, heart disease, COPD, kidney disease, immunosuppression)
Influenza virus strains change annually — new vaccine formulated each year
Available at private clinics; free at some government hospitals for priority groups
PNEUMOCOCCAL VACCINE (PCV 15/20 or PPSV23)
Recommended for: All adults >65 years; adults <65 with chronic illness or immunocompromise
PCV15 or PCV20 (conjugate): Preferred, especially for immunocompromised
PPSV23 (polysaccharide): May be given in combination depending on prior vaccination
Protects against Streptococcus pneumoniae: Leading cause of bacterial pneumonia, meningitis, sepsis
HEPATITIS B
3-dose series (0, 1, 6 months) if not vaccinated as a child or if seronegative
High priority for: Healthcare workers, people with multiple sexual partners, household contacts of HBsAg-positive persons, dialysis patients, PLHIV
Also prevents liver cancer (HBV is the leading cause of liver cancer in India)
HEPATITIS A
2-dose series (6–12 months apart)
Recommended for: Adults without prior infection or vaccination; travellers to endemic areas; chronic liver disease patients; food handlers
India is moving from high to intermediate endemicity — adult susceptibility increasing
HPV VACCINE (Human Papillomavirus)
Prevents cervical cancer and other HPV-associated cancers and genital warts
Recommended for: Females 9–45 years; males 9–26 years (for genital warts and anal cancer prevention)
Ideally administered before sexual debut (most effective), but beneficial across all age groups
2-dose series for ages 9–14; 3-dose series for ages 15 and above
CERVAVAC (India's indigenous HPV vaccine): Available in India; introduced into national immunisation programme in phased manner
Free at government health centres for adolescent girls (as per state-wise rollout)
VARICELLA (CHICKENPOX)
2 doses (4–8 weeks apart) for adults with no history of chickenpox and no prior vaccination
Chickenpox in adults is generally more severe than in children
Avoid in pregnancy — consult doctor
TYPHOID
Typhoid Conjugate Vaccine (TCV): Recommended for adults travelling to or living in high-risk areas, food handlers, lab workers
Booster every 2–3 years if risk continues
RABIES (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis)
3-dose pre-exposure series recommended for: Veterinarians, animal handlers, wildlife workers, laboratory personnel, frequent travellers to high-risk areas
Post-exposure: Any bite/scratch from animal — seek immediate medical care for post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) regardless of pre-exposure vaccination
MENINGOCOCCAL VACCINE
Recommended for: Pilgrims to Mecca (Hajj/Umrah) — mandatory; college students in dormitories; travel to meningitis belt (sub-Saharan Africa); asplenic patients; complement deficiency
HERPES ZOSTER (SHINGLES) VACCINE
Recommended for adults >60 years (preferably >65 years)
Prevents reactivation of varicella-zoster virus (shingles) — causes painful rash along nerve distribution
Two-dose recombinant subunit vaccine (RZV/Shingrix): Preferred; >90% effective
Currently available privately in India
Pregnancy:
RECOMMENDED: Tdap (every pregnancy, 27–36 weeks), Influenza (any trimester), Hepatitis B (if non-immune)
AVOID: Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, yellow fever) during pregnancy unless risk-benefit assessed
HIV / Immunocompromised individuals:
Need pneumococcal, influenza, hepatitis A and B, HPV vaccines as priorities
AVOID live vaccines in severe immunocompromise (discuss with doctor)
May need earlier/more doses (reduced immune response)
Chronic Disease (Diabetes, Heart Disease, Kidney Disease, COPD):
Influenza: Annually
Pneumococcal: One-time (with possible booster)
Hepatitis B: If not vaccinated
Healthcare Workers:
Hepatitis B: Mandatory (occupational exposure risk)
Influenza: Annual
Varicella: If non-immune
Tdap: Updated booster
MMR: If not immune
Elderly (>60 years):
Influenza: Annual
Pneumococcal: As per schedule
Td/Tdap: Booster
Herpes Zoster: Shingrix (2 doses)
Hepatitis B: If not vaccinated
Travel Vaccination:
Depends on destination country and activities
Consult a travel medicine specialist or government travel vaccination centre
Common travel vaccines: Hepatitis A, Typhoid, Yellow Fever (required for some countries), Meningococcal, Rabies pre-exposure, Japanese Encephalitis (for rural Southeast/South Asia)
Review vaccination records/card
For hepatitis B: Serology test (HBsAg, anti-HBs titre) to determine immunity
Consult a doctor — catch-up vaccination is possible for most vaccines at any age
Government vaccination records: Increasingly available via CoWIN (cowin.gov.in) — primarily COVID records
Universal Immunisation Programme (UIP): Free vaccines for infants and children; some adult vaccines (e.g. TT for pregnant women, COVID-19 vaccines)
CoWIN portal: cowin.gov.in
Nearest government hospital, PHC, or Urban Health Centre for publicly available adult vaccines
CERVAVAC HPV vaccine rollout: Check state health department for availability
Uncertain about vaccination history
Chronic illness, pregnancy, or immunocompromise — need individualised schedule
Travel planning internationally
Occupational exposure risk (healthcare, veterinary, food handling)
Before starting immunosuppressive therapy (chemotherapy, biologics, steroids)
Any adverse reaction after vaccination — report to doctor

treatment_awareness_only: Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Seek medical care for severe, persistent, worsening, or red-flag symptoms. Use emergency number 112 for life-threatening situations.
india_specific_context: Confirm current India-specific guidance through MoHFW, NHM, NHP, ICMR/NCDC, NIMHANS, or relevant state health services where applicable.

faqs.question: Which vaccines do adults need in India?
faqs.answer: Key adult vaccines include influenza (annual), Td/Tdap (every 10 years), Hepatitis B (if not vaccinated), and COVID-19 (as per current guidelines). High-risk groups may also need pneumococcal, HPV, and typhoid vaccines. Consult a doctor for a personalised schedule.

faqs.question: Are adult vaccines available free at government hospitals in India?
faqs.answer: Some adult vaccines are free at government hospitals (e.g., COVID-19, Td for pregnant women). Others like influenza and pneumococcal are available at private clinics. Check with your nearest PHC or government hospital for current availability.

faqs.question: Do I need a flu vaccine every year?
faqs.answer: Yes. Influenza viruses change each year, so the vaccine is reformulated annually to match circulating strains. Annual vaccination is recommended especially for elderly, pregnant women, healthcare workers, and those with chronic conditions.

faqs.question: I was vaccinated as a child. Do I still need adult boosters?
faqs.answer: Yes. Some childhood vaccines (like Td for tetanus/diphtheria) require boosters every 10 years. Others may wane over time. Consult a doctor to review your vaccination history and identify any gaps.
